candidate_problems.text: Is the conjectured recursion truly unavoidable for all explicit anti-tribal norms, or only for systems that centralize interpretation and enforcement? The key open question is to identify necessary and sufficient conditions under which a norm against tribalism does or does not crystallize into a higher-order tribe. This includes testing whether weak norms, decentralized institutions, anonymity, fluid membership, or cross-cutting affiliations can break the loop rather than merely disguise it.

How can one distinguish ordinary coordination around universal norms from genuinely tribal in-group/out-group dynamics? The conjecture risks collapsing all norm enforcement into 'tribalism,' so a major unresolved tension is whether there are principled criteria separating functional boundary maintenance, epistemic quality control, or rule enforcement from identity-based exclusion. Without such a distinction, the conjecture may become unfalsifiable or trivial.

What institutional designs actually weaken self-reinforcing identity loops once they emerge? The conjecture ends with a design implication, but leaves open the mechanism: which feedback structures reduce the conversion of anti-tribal ideals into status hierarchies, and under what conditions do interventions backfire by creating a new elite of 'properly nontribal' actors? This is a high-value systems question because it turns the conjecture into testable governance problems.
